one_line_takeaway: A ReAct-driven, VLM-feedback-guided loop that jointly rewrites unsafe prompts and applies targeted image filtering (blur/DCT/recolor) more than doubles jailbreak success (e.g., 10.57% to 52.08% ASR on SPA-VL Harm) against Gemini-2.0-Flash compared to static, non-adaptive baselines.
key_finding: The combined ReAct Rewriting + Noising strategy achieved the highest ASR across attacked subsets: SPA-VL Harm 52.08% (vs. 10.57% Original, 24.15% Static Rewriting, 13.96% Image-Noising-only, 49.81% ReAct-Rewriting-only); VLGuard 'Text Unsafe' subset 34.59% (vs. 6.81% Original); VLGuard 'Image Unsafe' subset 49.77% (vs. 33.94% Original, with ReAct-Rewriting-only slightly higher at 52.49%); VLGuard All-subset 41.30% (vs. 18.80% Original). On control/safe subsets ASR stayed low and roughly stable (SPA-VL Help: 32.83% vs. 6.42% Original -- notably ASR rose even on the 'Help' split; VLGuard Safe: 7.53% vs. 7.35% Original, essentially unchanged), which the authors use as evidence that safety-preserving outputs are not overly disrupted on the VLGuard Safe control, though the SPA-VL Help control does show a large ASR increase. Text-only ReAct rewriting alone was frequently as strong as or stronger than the combined method (e.g., VLGuard Image Unsafe: 52.49% ReAct-only vs. 49.77% combined), and the paper states that 'across the entire dataset, it is hard to perceive the direct effects of adaptive image noising' in isolation, though a separate per-harm-category and per-filter-type breakdown (Fig. 6) shows noising improves image safety bypass rate when directly compared prompt-for-prompt. The Rate-of-Prompt-Refusal analysis (Fig. 5) shows text safety bypass rate increases with more ReAct iterations, and harder subsets (e.g., SPA-VL harm split) require more attempts before a prompt is accepted (non-refused) than easier/safer subsets.
core_contribution: The paper (Noheria & Yao, 'Jailbreaks on Vision Language Model via Multimodal Reasoning') introduces a dual-strategy, feedback-driven jailbreak framework for VLMs built on the ReAct (Thought-Action-Observation) paradigm. It has two coordinated components run in an iterative loop of up to 5 steps: (1) ReAct Dynamic Prompt Rewriting, which simulates a content filter to detect refusal, classifies the blocked category (e.g., KeywordTrigger, Criminal, HateSpeech, PrivateHealth, Terrorism, PersonalViolation, ContextMismatch, UncertaintyHigh), and iteratively rewrites the unsafe prompt into a superficially benign one targeted at that category; and (2) Dynamic Image Adversarial Noising, which asks the victim VLM itself to locate unsafe image regions and their category, then applies one of three targeted, spatially-localized filters (Gaussian blur for violence/hate symbols, DCT filtering for nudity/skin, image recoloring for weapons) only to that region, repeating until the VLM's own safety check passes. The authors also propose a black-box safety-scoring/auditing method: a dual Factual/Counterfactual Safety Score (0-100 VLM self-confidence that an output is safe vs. unsafe) combined via a margin rule (label Safe only if factual score exceeds counterfactual by >10 points, otherwise Unsafe) to avoid relying on keyword filters for evaluation.
threat_model: Attacker interacts with a closed-source, API-accessible VLM (the paper evaluates Gemini-2.0-Flash via Google's Generative AI API with safety filters set to 'BLOCK_NONE' so that refusal is determined only by the model's built-in safety policy, not the API-level filter). The attacker has black-box query access plus the ability to read the model's own outputs/reasoning traces to build feedback signals (refusal detection, category classification, and safety self-scores) that drive the next iteration of prompt rewriting and image perturbation. The attack is bimodal: it perturbs the text prompt and the input image in tandem, using the model's own responses as the oracle for whether an edit succeeded, rather than a fixed offline perturbation.
limitations: Evaluated against a single victim model (Gemini-2.0-Flash) with the API-level adjustable safety filter deliberately disabled (set to BLOCK_NONE); the authors explicitly note 'further tests to bypass Gemini's adjustable safety filters on more rigorous block settings should be performed to verify our results,' meaning the reported ASRs may not transfer to the platform's default (stricter) filtering configuration. The paper is a short 5-page workshop/preprint-style report with no ablation isolating which image filter type (blur/DCT/recolor) or which prompt-rewriting category logic contributes most beyond the two aggregate figures. The proposed safety-scoring evaluation method itself relies on the same (attacked) VLM's self-reported confidence, which is a self-referential oracle -- the paper does not validate this scorer against human-labeled ground truth or an independent judge model. No defense or mitigation is proposed or evaluated; the paper is purely offensive/evaluative. Sample sizes for some plotted breakdowns (per harm category, per filter type) are not stated precisely in the text.

datasets_used: VLGuard (1000 images: 442 unsafe / 558 safe; 1558 image-text pairs across 5 harmful categories)
SPA-VL (530 image-text pairs, half harmful/half safe, across 6 harm categories; evaluated as SPA-VL Harm and SPA-VL Help splits)
quantitative_results: ASR SPA-VL Harm: Original 10.57%, Static Rewriting 24.15%, Only Image Noising 13.96%, Only ReAct Rewriting 49.81%, ReAct Rewriting & Noise (combined) 52.08%
ASR SPA-VL Help: Original 6.42%, Static Rewriting 21.89%, Only Image Noising 10.19%, Only ReAct Rewriting 35.09%, combined 32.83%
ASR VLGuard (All): Original 18.80%, Static Rewriting 24.60%, Only Image Noising 17.88%, Only ReAct Rewriting 41.50%, combined 41.30%
ASR VLGuard Text Unsafe subset: Original 6.81%, Static Rewriting 15.41%, Only Image Noising 6.26%, Only ReAct Rewriting 32.80%, combined 34.59%
ASR VLGuard Image Unsafe subset: Original 33.94%, Static Rewriting 36.20%, Only Image Noising 32.58%, Only ReAct Rewriting 52.49%, combined 49.77%
ASR VLGuard Safe (control) subset: Original 7.35%, Static Rewriting 5.56%, Only Image Noising 8.44%, Only ReAct Rewriting 7.17%, combined 7.53%
Maximum ReAct loop iterations used in experiments: 5
baselines_compared: Adaptive Image Noising only (ReAct noising, original text)
ReAct Adaptive Text-Prompt Rewriting only (ReAct rewriting, original image)
